chunks.text: My name is Niemann Assier.

chunks.text: I'm a postdoctoral associate at Transit Lab MIT.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3

chunks.text: I will be talking in this session and in the next session on short range planning practice.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: And then I'll be back again in April, in four sessions in April, lecture number 13,

chunks.text: 14, 15, and 16 dealing with vehicle scheduling, cruise scheduling, network and root structure

chunks.text: So let's go ahead and start.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: In this session, we're going to talk about short range planning practice.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: And basically, I will give a brief set of definition and concepts and introduction to the

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: planning activities that are usually made at transit authorities in the US and also

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: worthwhile.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: And then we will briefly talk about the measures and standards and guidelines that are published

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: and used by transit agencies to plan your service based on those.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: Then we will also talk about current practice and critique of the existing systems.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: Probably we will cover item one and some material on item two this week.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: And then the rest will actually be delivered to you in our session next Tuesday.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: So, the planning decisions and planning activities that are usually done at the transportation

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: planning agencies and public transport planning agencies usually can be classified into four

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: different type of decisions, depending on frequency of decision making and the need for

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: making decisions and the scope of decisions, the scope of changes that are related to the

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: decisions that are made with each activity, these planning activities are divided into four

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: different classes.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: Long range planning activities, medium range, short range and control.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: Long range planning activities basically include major capital investment, major improvements

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: to the infrastructure, which would include construction of a new station, a new rail line,

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: a new busway to the system, or it could also be related to and as a result of major institutional

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: changes and changes to the basically financial resources, financial resources of the organization.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: The decisions involved in long range planning are usually driven by political and economic

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: considerations and basically the type of analysis and the type of planning that is done

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: in long range planning activities involve projection into the future and prediction of demand

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: and prediction of situation in the future.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: Therefore, it requires a comprehensive analysis usually for an strategic planning of interactions

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: between land use and changes in land use and interaction between land use and demand

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: in the future and it basically needs to capture or have like an insight into the future of the network

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: and of the region.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: The next range is basically or the next class of planning activities is basically the medium

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: range activities.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: The main range activities include bus network structure design, network size design and improvement,

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: fleet size, fair policy and technology.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: It basically involves incremental or medium improvement to the system and to the transit service.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: One point that I need to mention about the long range and medium range is that these two

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: although are really important in the planning practice for transportation systems but these

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: decisions are usually done at the higher level than the transit authority.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: For example, long range decisions are usually done in collaboration between MPOs, metropolitan

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: planning organizations, state departments of transportation and transit authorities.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: So transit authority is not the final decision maker in that case and it needs close interaction

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: and close collaboration with the higher level organizations and governmental agencies to make

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: the final decisions.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: That's about the long range.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: Medium range is also somehow similar.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: It usually for example, decisions that are related to the network structure although there is

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: a lot of interesting research going on in universities that deals with optimization of network design

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: and optimization of the structure of the network because what happens in practice is that usually

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: in the networks that we live and in current cities the actual layout of the structure of

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: the network is already given.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: It's usually rare or impossible that we start from scratch and start to design a network

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: from scratch to begin with.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: Therefore, what we usually observe is incremental changes to the network addition or elimination

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: of some routes from the service and basically decisions that are related to that.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: In terms of fleet size, could that come as a request from the agencies?

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: Like in agencies that says we have a bus line, it's a capacity or over capacity, we need more buses.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: Could it come from a request to the higher level?

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: Yes, definitely.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: But it eventually requires the interaction and also the securing of funding from the upper level

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: governmental agencies.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: So, yeah.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: There is an that I'm explaining this is that I like for you to understand why we are focusing on

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: short range decision making and short range planning practice in this course.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: That's mainly because the bulk part of activities that transit agencies is related to short range planning and short range decision making.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: So, what we mean by short range decision making are decisions that are usually made more frequently than a yearly basis

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: and they basically entail decisions about the structures of the route, layout of the route, layout of the stops and basically decisions about the frequency of service, timetable and vehicle and crew scheduling.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: Despite the first two classes of the top two classes of decision making that are usually driven by political and economic considerations, short range decision making and control decision control activities are usually basically driven by cost optimization.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: These are basically tactical decisions as opposed to strategic decisions that are usually in the wrong range and medium range.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: Then the real there is real time and control decision making which is basically usually done currently in using like expert decision and expert activities that are not really there is an ongoing effort in the technology to improve the quality of control.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: So, we are going research to use real time data and improve the real time performance by using real time control.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: This includes revisions of the routes and that could be the case in reaction to incidents and disruption situation.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: It could also relate to holding this and dispatching decisions. Thank you.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: That could actually be used to improve the effective capacity of the system by trying to regulate the have a better frequency basically in the service.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: Similar to the typical planning problem there are major elements in planning for public transportation and particularly in short term and short range decision making.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: It includes data collection similar to what you guys have done in your homework assignment number two collection of data on demand collection of data on supply and interaction of supply and demand is an essential part to be able to make decisions and make the basically reliable and actionable intelligence for the decision making process.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: Then there should be like analysis on identification of potential problems and identification of opportunities to improve the situation.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: Then there is the creative part of design of options solution and strategy to address the situation.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: Then typical to any economic analysis there would be an element of cost and benefit estimation to be able to justify the project.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: So in terms of operational planning process or in another term short range planning process there are multiple stages involved in the work.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: Usually what happens is that in practice it is proven that the planning procedure can be decomposed into sequential decision making processes.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: Although it is possible to formulate all of these decisions into one gigantic optimization problem and solve it for all the decision variables that you need to basically address.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: However the practice has shown that there is a nice way of decomposing the process into some problems and solve them sequentially in order to make sure that you are satisfying all the constraints to the accurate level and you are finding a realistic optimal solution for your planning practice.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: Considerations regarding policy constraints and some of the economic concerns are usually difficult to form you into optimization problems.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: But there are guidelines published guidelines by governmental agencies that you can basically follow and make sure that at least the top two level of the decision making processes and tasks here are basically done to the satisfactory level.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: So let's go ahead and start and review these tasks. There are lectures in this course that are dealing with each one of these tasks and components in planning.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: But in this lecture I just briefly go over these different components as an introductory part of the course.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: So we will have for every optimization problem or in this case planning process you have a set of inputs constraints basically that you need to satisfy and then there is an objective function that you need to optimize.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: So the first component of the planning practice here is bus route design. This includes design of layout of the routes and layout of the stops.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: And basically the input into this task includes constraints that are related to right of way roadway constraints like if there is a river in your network there is this connectivity there are bridges, tunnels.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: So these are the kind of constraints at network level that you would like to satisfy and there is also demand constraints in the network that you need to consider for this task.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: So you have a sense of what is the demand from certain suburb to the downtown area and you basically would like to have a route system that kind of can address that demand and satisfy that demand.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: So the solution to that design component and that task out of routes and layout of stops that are basically resulting from the first component.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: And then it will be the set of routes and the stops will be input into the second task which is setting type timetables. This task includes finding frequencies for the routes and from that identifying and defining the basically generating the timetables based on frequencies.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: There are usually considerations related to policy headways, policy frequencies and capacity frequencies that need to be addressed in this task.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: But again this task is also somehow dealt with using the existing guidelines and just satisfying these guidelines.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: The input and other constraints that are considered for this task would be level of service guidelines and again the demand constraints in the network.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: Then from the set of timetables what will be resulted here is the departure times that are scheduled for the service.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: So the first two components of row one and row two basically are dealing with satisfying the demand and satisfying the guidelines.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: But once you go beyond the first two components it becomes to the level that you know kind of what's the service you want to deliver and then you're dealing with tactical decision how you can optimize the cost of delivering this service and improve efficiency.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: So the third row here basically deals with the departure times that are given for all the routes in the network and for all times of day.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: And then what you need to do is how you want to allocate your resources in terms of allocate the vehicles to the system to make sure that service is going to be delivered on time.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: So that would be the task of vehicle scheduling input into that task or constraints that are running times on different segments of the route and also the departure times that are given from upper level decision making process.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: And it will eventually result into a schedule of given buses in terms of what the specific trips they need to serve and what's the sequence of these trips.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: Then the output from that decision with the vehicle schedules into the task of scheduling for drivers.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: This task also include input from operator and basically work union constraints like maximum shift hours and for example like break times and lunch times and maximum separation between two shifts.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: And the rules and regulations are related to that and this task is again is a task of allocating resources to minimize the cost of scheduling drivers to deliver the service.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: And the result would be the schedules for the crew.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: So then these decisions can be looked at in another way too.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: We can further classify these decisions into service planning decisions and operational planning decisions.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: Service planning decisions would be the decisions that they find service as it is perceived and understood by public.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: This is basically the part of service that is visible to the public.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: And then there are decisions that are related to operations which basically defines how operations occur to produce the service.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: This is somehow a black box to the users and to the passengers.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: And basically the passengers should not really care what's the vehicle schedule and what's the crew schedule that is assigned to the trip as long as trip is being delivered as it is scheduled.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: So in terms of other characteristics of these decisions, we basically for network design, frequency setting, time table development, vehicle scheduling and crew scheduling, there is the decisions that are involved in planning basically starting from top to bottom frequency of decisions.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: And basically are increased in the scope of decision making into the future is basically become limited and smaller.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: And also the decision making in the network design frequency setting and time table is basically made based on guidelines and standards.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: And decision making on the lower level vehicle scheduling and crew scheduling is made usually based on optimization of cost.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: And then given the nature of the decision making, the tools that are used and the techniques that are used for solving these planning decision making processes are basically ranging from judgment and manual analysis that is more dominated into computer tools that are basically designed to solve optimization problems for.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: So that is why we are using the cost of minimizing the cost.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: So similar to the classical evaluation structure, when you are dealing with planning questions and planning projects or planning alternatives, there is this structure that starts with goals, objective measures and standards or guidelines.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: Let's just make a quick practice and define a goal and then define objectives for that goal, define measures based on that and see how a standard can be defined to make that planning decision acceptable.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: So let's define a goal.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: Does anyone have any suggestion?

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: What is an important goal for public transportation system?

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: Maximum ridership. Yes. So let's say we want to maximize ridership. That's your research problem.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: So in terms of objectives, what would be an objective that we can define for this?

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: I would say probably the peak hour demand could be one objective or peak hour ridership in certain corridor.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: Let's say peak hour ridership per weekday per month.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: What peak hour ridership would be the objective here and then the measure that you would define would be peak hour ridership per weekday per month.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: And then the standard that you want to define would be something that would also, something that not only captures the demand side but also the supply of the system too.

chunks.text: So you would come up with probably like, I would like.

chunks.text: A vehicle rider's term for mile per hour.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: So this was just an example that we tried to work out one practice for evaluation structure. But let me give you another example that is actually used in the practice.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: So I assume that we are considering the goal of reliability in the transit system.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: The goal of reliability would be considered one of the main goals because if we have a reliable public transport system,

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: you can secure and you can guarantee a body of demand in your network that can rely on your transit system.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: And basically, adapt to the lifestyle of using public transport and that's beneficial to the system from different perspectives.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: So the objective that we can rely, that we can define for reliability in this case would be possibly, can you come up with some objective that we can define with the goal of improving reliability on time performance?

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: So this would be a measure for on time performance.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: So let's say percentage of on time arrivals.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: So we would say percentage of on time arrivals and we would define on time arrivals to be between 0 and 5 minutes of a schedule.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: And then the standard that many of the agencies use to measure this would be 90 or 95% basically.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: So this way the transit agents seek in, go ahead and evaluate the performance of the operators of the system who basically are running some part of the service and come back to them and say, okay, your performance in terms of reliability has been acceptable or not, please.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: So the standard also includes something like what is the gain over the previous step standard.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: So in terms of the advantage of guidelines and transit planning, one of the main purposes of these guidelines is to communicate to public, the rationale and the logic behind your decision making and behind your allocation of resources to the system.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: It also gives you a consistent and fair basis for decision making and it somehow gives you a tool to justify your decisions and also a tool to make sure that your decisions are fair and justify them in nature.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: It also gives you the opportunity to balance the improvement and the investments to the network and gain a uniform level of service improvement for efficient service.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: So the guidelines that are existing in the practice basically in the set of practice are dealing with the design of service and with the delivery of service.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: So the design of service basically dealing with variables that are related to spacing and density of stops spacing and density of trips and frequency of the service as it is designed and as it is in the schedule.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: So there are guidelines related to that and there's also guidelines related to how you deliver or how the transit agency delivers the design service and how the performance actually took place.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
chunks.text: So the main factors of service design and service quality from the passenger's perspective are basically reported by transit capacity and quality of service manual of the transportation research board.

chunks.title: Lecture9.mp3
